1: theme Bless the LORD, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name! The whole-person blessing of God — realized in Christ who loves the Lord with all his heart, soul, mind, and strength (Mark 12:30), perfectly fulfilling what Israel could only approximate. The holy name blessed here is the name Jesus declares to his Father: I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me (John 17:6).
2: theme Bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits, Forgetting none of God's benefits — the Eucharist as the church's act of remembrance: do this in remembrance of me (1 Corinthians 11:24-25). Christ institutes the meal precisely so his benefits — forgiveness, body broken, blood poured — are not forgotten. Every celebration of the Lord's Supper is the soul blessing God and forgetting none of his benefits.
3: direct who forgives all your iniquity, who heals all your diseases, Forgiveness and healing united — the signature of Christ's ministry. Matthew 9:2-7: Jesus forgives the paralytic's sins, and when challenged asks, which is easier, to say your sins are forgiven, or to say rise and walk? — then heals him to prove the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins. The conjunction of forgiveness and healing in Psalms 103:3 is precisely enacted in Christ.
4: shadow who redeems your life from the pit, who crowns you with steadfast love and mercy, Redemption from the pit — the resurrection as the ultimate pit-redemption. Christ descended into death (Ephesians 4:9) and was raised out of the pit: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it (Acts 2:24). Those in him are crowned with the steadfast love and mercy Christ absorbed — through him we have obtained access by faith into this grace (Romans 5:2).
5: shadow who satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's. Youth renewed like the eagle — the resurrection transformation. If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you (Romans 8:11). The renewal of youth is not cosmetic but ontological — the new creation body inaugurated in Christ's resurrection.
6: shadow The LORD works righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed. Righteousness and justice for the oppressed — the Nazareth manifesto: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to set at liberty those who are oppressed (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1). Christ is the LORD working righteousness for the oppressed, fulfilling the prophetic vision of Psalms 103:6 in his own person and ministry.
7: type He made known his ways to Moses, his acts to the people of Israel. God's ways known to Moses — fulfilled and surpassed in Christ: the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ (John 1:17). The mediator of the law who spoke with God face to face (Exodus 33:11) is superseded by the one who is the face of God (2 Corinthians 4:6) and the mediator of the new covenant (Hebrews 9:15).
8: revelation of God The LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love. The covenant name of God (Exodus 34:6-7) — which Christ makes visible. God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ (Ephesians 2:4-5). The divine character defined in Psalms 103:8 is the character displayed at the cross and in the resurrection.
9: shadow He will not always chide, nor will he keep his anger forever. The anger that does not last forever — the atonement makes this possible without compromising justice. God's wrath is satisfied in Christ (Romans 3:25 — propitiation) so that he does not chide forever. In overflowing anger for a moment I hid my face from you, but with everlasting love I will have compassion on you (Isaiah 54:8) — the moment of the cross gives way to everlasting compassion.
10: shadow He does not deal with us according to our sins, nor repay us according to our iniquities. Grace exceeding deserved punishment — the doctrine of justification. For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:21). God does not deal with believers according to their sins because Christ was dealt with according to those sins on their behalf. The not-dealing is grounded in the substitution.
11: revelation of God For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love toward those who fear him; The immeasurable love — Paul reaches for the same cosmic scale: I pray that you, being rooted and grounded in love, may have strength to comprehend with all the saints what is the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge (Ephesians 3:17-19). The heavens-above-earth image is the inadequate metaphor Paul tries to exceed.
12: shadow as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us. Total removal of transgressions — the irreversible forgiveness grounded in Christ's atonement. Paul's declaration: there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). The infinite east-west distance is the distance the cross places between the believer and their sin. He has delivered us from the domain of darkness (Colossians 1:13) — a complete removal.
13: direct As a father shows compassion to his children, so the LORD shows compassion to those who fear him; The fatherly compassion enacted in Christ's parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:20): But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him. Jesus gives the parable to reveal the Father's heart — the running father of Luke 15 is the LORD of Psalms 103:13 made visible in a story.
14: shadow For he knows our frame; he remembers that we are dust. God knowing human frailty — the Incarnation is God not merely remembering but entering: since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things (Hebrews 2:14). Christ knows our frame from the inside — he grew tired (John 4:6), wept (John 11:35), hungered (Matthew 4:2), and died. His knowing is not theoretical but experiential.
15: shadow As for man, his days are like grass; he flourishes like a flower of the field; Human transience like grass — 1 Peter 1:24 applies Isaiah 40:6-8 (same image) to the contrast between the fading flesh and the enduring word of God. Christ enters the transience of grass-like humanity and transforms it: his resurrection body is the firstfruits of the imperishable (1 Corinthians 15:42-44), the flowering of humanity that does not fade.
16: shadow for the wind passes over it, and it is gone, and its place knows it no more. The passing wind and vanishing life — the transience that Christ redeems. Your life is a vapor that appears for a little time and then vanishes (James 4:14). But those in Christ do not merely vanish: For this corruptible must put on the incorruptible, and this mortal must put on immortality (1 Corinthians 15:53). The wind that blew Jesus into the tomb also came as the breath of the resurrection morning.
17: revelation of God But the steadfast love of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear him, and his righteousness to children's children, Everlasting steadfast love — the love of God from which nothing can separate: I am sure that neither death nor life... nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord (Romans 8:38-39). The from-everlasting-to-everlasting love is the love that sent the Son (John 3:16) and keeps all who are in him.
18: shadow to those who keep his covenant and remember to do his commandments. Covenant-keeping as the condition of receiving steadfast love — fulfilled in Christ the perfect covenant-keeper. He kept every commandment (John 8:29 — I always do the things that are pleasing to him) and his obedience is imputed to those who are in him (Romans 5:19 — by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous).
19: direct The LORD has established his throne in the heavens, and his kingdom rules over all. The heavenly throne and universal kingdom — Christ's enthronement: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matthew 28:18). Revelation places Christ on the heavenly throne (Revelation 3:21; Psalms 5:6-14) alongside the Father. The kingdom that rules over all is now the kingdom of the Son (Colossians 1:13 — the kingdom of his beloved Son).
20: shadow Bless the LORD, O you his angels, you mighty ones who do his word, obeying the voice of his word! Angels obeying the voice of God's word — Christ is the Word (John 1:1). At his birth the angels proclaimed him (Luke 2:13-14); at the temptation they ministered to him (Matthew 4:11); at the resurrection they announced him (Matthew 28:2-7). The mighty angels who obey the voice of his word now obey the voice of the incarnate Word.
21: shadow Bless the LORD, all his hosts, his ministers who do his will! All the heavenly hosts blessing and serving — the throne-room of Revelation: ten thousand times ten thousand and thousands of thousands, saying with a loud voice, Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing! (Revelation 5:11-12). The hosts who bless God bless him through the Lamb.
22: theme Bless the LORD, all his works, in all places of his dominion. Bless the LORD, O my soul! All creation blessing the LORD — the eschatological worship of Revelation 5:13: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! The Psalm's universal praise reaches its target: Christ and the Father, receiving the blessing of all creation.
